steps.specialNote: الْحَقُّ (marfu — subject/predicate) vs بِالْحَقِّ (majroor — after preposition). Watch how the ending changes based on the word's role in the sentence.

ayahCards.arabicText: الْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّكَ فَلَا تَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْمُمْتَرِينَ
ayahCards.translation: The truth is from your Lord, so do not be among the doubters.
He has sent down upon you the Book in truth.

steps.specialNote: يَوْمَ الدِّينِ (day of judgement) — يَوْم here is majroor (genitive) because it is the first word of an Idhafah. You say this in every prayer in Al-Fatiha.

ayahCards.translation: Master of the Day of Judgement.

ayahCards.arabicText: وَمِنَ النَّاسِ مَن يَقُولُ آمَنَّا بِاللَّهِ وَبِالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ
ayahCards.translation: And among the people are some who say, 'We believe in Allah and the Last Day.'

steps.question: الْحَقُّ ends in ـُ (damma). What case is this?
steps.explanation: The three cases: Marfu (ـُ) for subjects/predicates, Mansoob (ـَ) for objects, Majroor (ـِ) after prepositions or in Idhafah. Arabic grammar is expressed through these endings.

steps.question: بِالْحَقِّ ends in ـِ (kasra). What case is this, and why?
choices: Majroor (genitive) — the preposition بِ causes it
No case — it is a verb
steps.explanation: Prepositions (like بِ, فِي, مِن, عَلَى) always put the noun after them into the Majroor case — shown by the kasra ending ـِ.
